name: Amaterasu
description: Amaterasu-ōmikami, meaning "Great Divinity Illuminating Heaven," is the sun goddess in Japanese mythology and one of the most important Shinto deities. She is the ruler of Takamagahara (the High Celestial Plain), the realm of the kami. Born from the left eye of Izanagi, her birth was a miraculous event that brought light and warmth to the world. Amaterasu possesses the power to illuminate the world and bestow life-giving energy. Her most famous myth involves her retreating into the Ama-no-Iwato (Heavenly Rock Cave) after a conflict with her brother Susanoo, the storm god, plunging the world into darkness. The other gods devised a plan to lure her out, using a mirror and a boisterous dance by Ame-no-Uzume. Her role is paramount, as she is considered the ancestor of the Japanese Imperial Family, legitimizing their rule. She is venerated for her benevolence and her role in ensuring the prosperity and well-being of Japan. Her presence brings sunlight, warmth, and fertility to the land. Amaterasu's influence extends to agriculture, as her blessings ensure bountiful harvests. She is also a symbol of peace, harmony, and the importance of light in overcoming darkness. Temples dedicated to her are found throughout Japan, the most important being the Ise Grand Shrine. She is often depicted as a radiant and benevolent figure, embodying the essence of the sun.
subtitle: Sun Goddess and Ancestor of Emperors

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Amaterasu
keyMyths.description: Born from Izanagi's left eye, Amaterasu's birth brought light and warmth to the world, establishing her as a powerful and benevolent deity.

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu in the Rock Cave
keyMyths.description: After a conflict with Susanoo, Amaterasu hid in the Ama-no-Iwato, plunging the world into darkness until the other gods lured her out with a mirror and dance.

keyMyths.title: The Descent of Ninigi
keyMyths.description: Amaterasu sent her grandson Ninigi to rule Japan, entrusting him with the sacred mirror, sword, and jewel, symbols of imperial authority.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Offering of food and textiles
worshipPractices.description: Amaterasu is frequently offered food, especially rice, and beautiful textiles as a sign of respect and to ensure continued blessings. These offerings are often placed before her enshrined representation in Shinto shrines, symbolizing gratitude for her life-giving light.

worshipPractices.name: Purification rituals
worshipPractices.description: Purity is essential in Shinto, and rituals are performed to cleanse oneself before approaching Amaterasu or her shrine. These rituals can involve washing hands and mouth with water, symbolizing the removal of impurities and allowing for a respectful connection with the goddess.

festivals.name: Shōgatsu (New Year)
festivals.description: As the sun goddess, Amaterasu is implicitly honored during Shōgatsu, the most important festival in Japan. The holiday emphasizes renewal and good fortune, aligning with the sun's promise of a new day and the hope for a prosperous year under her benevolent gaze.

festivals.name: Ise Grand Shrine Festivals
festivals.description: Ise Grand Shrine, dedicated to Amaterasu, hosts numerous festivals throughout the year that celebrate her divinity. These festivals involve elaborate rituals, traditional music and dance, and processions to honor the sun goddess and ensure the well-being of Japan.

cultural.modernLegacy: Amaterasu's influence is visible in the Japanese flag, the Hinomaru, which represents the sun and her divine lineage. She continues to inspire art, literature, and popular culture, often portrayed as a powerful and benevolent force of nature. Her story and imagery are also frequently used in video games and anime, solidifying her enduring presence in modern Japan.

companions.name: Ame-no-Uzume
companions.description: Ame-no-Uzume is the goddess of dawn, mirth, and revelry. She famously danced provocatively outside the cave where Amaterasu hid, luring the sun goddess out and restoring light to the world.

companions.name: Futotama
companions.description: Futotama is a deity associated with the rituals performed to coax Amaterasu from her cave. He held a sacred mirror during the ceremony, reflecting her own radiant image back at her.

companions.name: Kushinadahime
companions.description: Kushinadahime was protected by Amaterasu's brother, Susanoo, from the Yamata no Orochi. While not directly related to Amaterasu, Susanoo's actions were linked to her, and she is generally supportive of Susanoo.

companions.name: Yata no Kagami
companions.description: The Yata no Kagami is the sacred mirror used to lure Amaterasu out of the cave. It is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing wisdom and honesty, and is intrinsically linked to her story.

companions.name: Ame-no-Koyane
companions.description: Ame-no-Koyane is a deity who recited prayers and incantations to encourage Amaterasu to emerge from the cave. His words played a crucial role in the ritual.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu's birth is a pivotal moment in Japanese mythology, marking the advent of light and order into the cosmos. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the two primary written sources of Japanese mythology, she emerged from the left eye of Izanagi, the creator god, as he purified himself after his descent into Yomi, the underworld, to retrieve his deceased wife, Izanami. This act of purification gave rise to three noble children: Amaterasu (sun), Tsukuyomi (moon, born from the right eye), and Susano-o (storm, born from his nose). These deities were tasked with governing the heavens, the night, and the seas, respectively.

The circumstances of Amaterasu's birth are significant. Unlike the other deities who emerged from physical actions or body parts associated with less sacred functions, her emergence from Izanagi's eye signifies her pure and celestial nature. This divine birthright establishes her as the supreme deity and ruler of Takamagahara, the High Celestial Plain. The act of Izanagi washing himself also highlights the importance of ritual purity in Shinto, a practice that continues to be observed in shrines and daily life.

The relationship between Amaterasu and her siblings, particularly Susano-o, is central to her mythos. Their interactions, often fraught with conflict, demonstrate the ongoing struggle between order and chaos in the cosmos. Susano-o's disruptive behavior eventually leads to the famous episode of Amaterasu's retreat into the Ama-no-Iwato cave, plunging the world into darkness. This incident underscores Amaterasu's vital role in maintaining the world's balance and the dire consequences of her absence.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu is one of the most widely worshipped deities in Shinto, and her principal shrine is the Ise Grand Shrine (Ise Jingū) in Mie Prefecture, considered the most sacred Shinto shrine in Japan. The Inner Shrine (Naiku) is dedicated to Amaterasu, housing the Sacred Mirror (Yata no Kagami), one of the Three Imperial Regalia, symbolizing her divine presence and the emperor's connection to the sun goddess. Rebuilding of Ise Shrine occurs every 20 years in a ritual called Shikinen Sengu, ensuring the preservation of traditional architectural techniques and the renewal of the shrine's spiritual power.

Worship of Amaterasu extends beyond Ise Shrine. Many smaller shrines throughout Japan are dedicated to her or incorporate her worship within their broader pantheon. Rituals and festivals honoring Amaterasu often involve elaborate dances, music, and offerings, all aimed at invoking her blessings and ensuring the continued prosperity of the community. These rituals reflect the importance of agriculture and the sun's role in sustaining life.

The Imperial family's claimed descent from Amaterasu further reinforces her significance in Japanese culture and history. This connection provides a divine mandate for the imperial lineage and underscores the emperor's role as a mediator between the celestial realm and the earthly world. The reverence for Amaterasu is deeply embedded in Japanese national identity, shaping cultural values and influencing social structures.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu is rarely depicted in a direct, anthropomorphic form. Instead, she is most commonly represented by the Sacred Mirror (Yata no Kagami), which embodies her radiant spirit and serves as a focal point of worship. The mirror is not merely a reflection of her image but a representation of her very essence, symbolizing honesty, purity, and self-reflection. This aniconic representation is characteristic of many early Shinto deities, reflecting a belief in the immanence of the divine within natural objects and phenomena.

When depicted anthropomorphically, Amaterasu is typically portrayed as a radiant and benevolent female figure, often dressed in elegant robes and adorned with solar motifs. She may be shown holding a fan or a sacred jewel, symbols of her authority and power. However, such representations are less common than the symbolic representation through the mirror. Images of Amaterasu often emphasize her association with light and warmth, reflecting her role as the sun goddess and the source of life.

The symbolism associated with Amaterasu extends beyond her visual representations. The color white, symbolizing purity and cleanliness, is often associated with her and is prominently featured in Shinto rituals and shrines dedicated to her. The rising sun, a powerful image in Japanese culture, is also directly linked to Amaterasu and represents new beginnings, hope, and national pride.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu's role in the cosmic order is paramount; she is not only the sun goddess but also the ruler of Takamagahara, the High Celestial Plain, the realm of the kami. Her light is essential for the growth of crops, the well-being of humans, and the overall balance of the world. The myth of her retreat into the Ama-no-Iwato cave vividly illustrates this importance. When she hides, darkness engulfs the world, plants wither, and chaos reigns. Only through the combined efforts of the other kami, who perform a raucous dance and display the jewel Yasakani no Magatama and the mirror Yata no Kagami, is Amaterasu lured out, restoring light and order.

Amaterasu's control over the sun also gives her influence over time and the seasons. Her benevolent nature ensures the cyclical return of light and warmth, essential for agricultural cycles and the continuation of life. This connection to the natural world reinforces the Shinto emphasis on harmony with nature and the importance of respecting the kami who govern it.

Furthermore, Amaterasu's role extends to the earthly realm through her descendants, the Imperial family. The belief in the Imperial lineage's divine origin from Amaterasu provides a strong foundation for the emperor's authority and underscores the interconnectedness between the celestial and earthly realms. This divine mandate reinforces the emperor's role as a symbol of national unity and continuity, deeply intertwined with the sun goddess's enduring influence.

shortDescription: Amaterasu is the sun goddess in Shinto mythology, ruler of Takamagahara, and a central figure in Japanese creation myths. She embodies light, warmth, and order.
